chunk_summary: Guidelines for multi-food elimination diets emphasize minimizing nutritional risks through defined endpoints, dietitian involvement, growth monitoring, and planned reintroduction to prevent malnutrition and system failure.
learning_objectives: Understand the risks and management strategies for multi-food elimination diets.
Learn to establish stop-loss rules and monitoring protocols to prevent malnutrition.
Identify appropriate durations and diagnostic endpoints for elimination diets in conditions like EoE.
Apply protein and energy anchor principles to ensure dietary adequacy.
key_facts: Multi-food elimination diets are high-risk and require a written rationale and defined endpoints.
Diagnostic non-IgE elimination diets typically last 2–6 weeks, while EoE diets may last 8–12 weeks before repeat endoscopy.
Growth reviews should occur within 2–4 weeks of starting the diet, with weight trends and dietary adequacy documented at each visit.
Laboratory assessments (e.g., hemoglobin, albumin, ferritin, vitamin B12) are triggered by high risk or symptoms of protein-losing disease.
Every multi-food elimination diet must include a written reintroduction plan to avoid system failure.
Protein and energy anchors are critical to prevent reliance on refined carbohydrates and ensure nutritional adequacy.
reference_quote: Multi-food elimination should be treated as a high-risk intervention because nutritional compromise is predictable. (ESPGHAN, 2024)
document_key_topics: Multi-food elimination diet risks and management
